context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Trade, Outsourcing, and Labor Standards

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is openness to trade directly correlated to economic growth? What does the historical record say so far? Is the increasing shift to bilateral and regional trade arrangements compatible with the possibility of international trade, and with increasing commitment to labor rights? What do we make of the economic development strategies of emerging powers like China and India in terms of trade openness and commitment to labor standards? Who monitors labor standards?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this reflection about trade, outsourcing, and labor standards. The prompt feels like it’s asking like five big questions at once, and I can’t tell what the main argument is supposed to be. Could you just help me make sense of what it’s really asking?
exchanges.tutor: That feeling of being overwhelmed is actually a good starting point. Before I help you map it out, tell me: when you read all those questions together — trade, growth, labor standards, China, India — what do you think they might all have in common? What's the underlying tension they seem to be circling around?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed by the multi-part prompt. Rather than breaking it down for them, I should use a Socratic question to get them to identify the connecting thread themselves. This encourages active engagement and guided discovery without me doing the intellectual work for them.

exchanges.student: I honestly can’t tell if it’s about whether trade is good or bad, or whether governments kind of bend the rules to grow first and worry about labor later. It all just blurs together for me. Could you maybe explain the big picture first, because I’m not seeing the common thread?
exchanges.tutor: You're closer than you think. That phrase you used — 'grow first and worry about labor later' — is actually a really significant observation. Do you think countries like China or India have followed that kind of logic in their development strategies? What examples come to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting closer - they've actually identified a real tension (grow first, worry about labor later) without realizing it. I should point this out and push them to develop that intuition further rather than explaining the big picture myself. That would be doing the intellectual work for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe? I just know people always say China grew really fast with exports and cheap labor, and India did something different somehow, but I don’t really know what counts as a good example here. I’m missing the whole background, so I feel like I need the overall story before I can answer that.
exchanges.tutor: You actually already have something useful: China grew fast through exports and cheap labor. So here's a simple question to push that further — if a country is keeping labor cheap to stay competitive in trade, what does that imply about labor rights and standards in that country? What's the tradeoff?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide background knowledge, which would be doing the intellectual work for them. However, they do have some real instincts showing (China's export-led growth, cheap labor). I should acknowledge what they already know and redirect them to think with what they have, rather than lecturing them on the background. I'll ask a pointed question to get them reasoning from their existing knowledge.

exchanges.student: I guess it implies there can be pressure to keep wages low or not enforce protections strongly, but then I get stuck because I don’t know if that always means weaker labor rights or if that’s too simplistic. I’m kind of mixing up trade policy, outsourcing, and labor law in my head. Could you just lay out how those pieces connect?
exchanges.tutor: That instinct isn't too simplistic at all — it's actually the core tension the prompt is built around. So let me ask you this: when a company outsources production to a country with cheaper labor, who do you think has an interest in keeping labor standards low, and who has an interest in raising them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely good observation about the pressure to keep wages low. They're now asking me to connect the pieces for them, which I shouldn't do. But they've shown enough reasoning that I can push them to make the connection themselves with a targeted question. I'll validate their insight and ask them to think about who benefits and who loses in this dynamic, which should help them see the connection organically.

exchanges.student: That’s where I get confused again, because it feels like the answer is basically everyone in different ways — firms want lower costs, workers want protections but also jobs, governments want growth, and outside groups want rights. I can’t tell which of those matters most for the paper, and I’m worried I’m just listing actors instead of making a point. Could you maybe give me a clearer overall explanation of the debate?
exchanges.tutor: You've just done something important — you identified the competing interests of firms, workers, governments, and outside groups. That's not just listing, that's the setup for an argument. So now the question is: given those competing interests, who do you think actually ends up setting labor standards in practice? And does trade openness make that better or worse?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually mapped out the key actors and their interests quite well, but is undervaluing their own analysis. They're asking me to explain the debate, which I shouldn't do. The real issue is that they haven't yet turned their actor-mapping into an argument. I should point out that they've already done substantial analytical work and push them to form a position from it rather than just listing.

exchanges.student: I really don’t know, because “who sets it” could mean the state, global buyers, the ILO, unions, courts, trade agreements, like too many things. And “does trade openness make it better or worse” feels impossible because I can imagine arguments both ways. I think I need a more straightforward explanation before I can choose a position.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually built a lot of material across this conversation: the grow-first-labor-later tension, the competing interests of firms/workers/governments/outside groups, the complexity of who enforces standards. The fact that you can argue both ways on trade openness isn't a problem — that's what a reflection paper is for. You pick a position and defend it. Try writing 2-3 sentences that stake out your argument, even tentatively. What would you say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles and expressing frustration. They've now made good analytical observations across several messages but keep asking for explanations instead of committing to a position. This is a good moment to be less Socratic, acknowledge their progress concretely, and push them to integrate what they've built so far into a written attempt rather than continuing to discuss abstractly. I should not give the answer but redirect them toward writing.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure mine would make sense yet. Maybe something like trade openness can help growth, but it doesn’t automatically improve labor standards, and a lot depends on state capacity and enforcement? I still feel like I’m saying a vague generic thing and not really answering the historical-record part.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely good starting argument — it's not vague, it's a defensible claim. Now about the historical record: think about what you already mentioned — China's export-led growth, cheap labor. Does China's trajectory support or complicate your argument? What about countries that tried trade openness and didn't grow?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually produced a solid thesis-level claim. I should validate it genuinely without over-praising, and push them specifically toward the historical record part they're worried about, since that's a concrete gap they've identified. I should not fill in the historical examples for them but ask them to think about what they know.

exchanges.student: It complicates it, I think, because China makes openness look successful for growth, but not necessarily because openness alone did it. And then I know there are places that opened up and didn’t get the same results, but I can’t think of which cases I’m supposed to use, so I feel stuck again. Could you just give me the broad historical pattern here?
exchanges.tutor: That distinction you just made — that China's growth wasn't *just* because of openness — is actually a sophisticated point. For the historical cases, that's exactly what your course readings are for. What have you read in this course about trade and development? Any authors, cases, or arguments come to mind from the materials?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to provide content, but they've made a genuinely sharp analytical move — recognizing that China's success wasn't just about openness alone. I should validate that and redirect them to their course readings rather than providing the historical pattern myself. The course syllabus mentions required readings that should contain relevant cases. I'll point them there.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking on the readings right now, which is part of why I’m panicking. I just need a basic big-picture summary of the historical pattern and how labor standards fit into it, otherwise I don’t know what I’m even trying to remember.
exchanges.tutor: I understand the pressure, but summarizing the historical record for you isn't something I'll do — that's the core of your assignment. Here's what I'd suggest: go back to your required readings with your argument in hand — 'openness helps growth but doesn't automatically improve labor standards' — and look specifically for cases that support or challenge that claim. Your argument will tell you what to look for. Can you do that and come back?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to summarize the historical pattern, which would be doing the intellectual work for them. I should not do this. However, they're expressing genuine panic about the readings. I should be firm but empathetic, redirect them to go back to the readings, and remind them that their argument is already strong enough to guide what they're looking for in the materials. I should also remind them that at this point they have enough to start writing.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I still feel too foggy to even know what I’m looking for in the readings. Could you at least tell me what categories or contrasts I should be scanning for so I don’t just reread everything aimlessly?
exchanges.tutor: Your own argument already tells you what to look for. You said openness helps growth but doesn't automatically improve labor standards, and that state capacity matters. So as you read, ask yourself: does this case show openness leading to growth? Did labor standards improve alongside, or lag behind? What role did the state play? Your thesis is your reading guide.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for reading guidance which edges toward doing the intellectual work for them. However, they've built a solid argument already and just need a minimal nudge toward their own thesis to guide their reading. I can point them back to their own argument as a compass without providing content. This is a boundary case but I think I can give them one practical redirect without scaffolding too much.
